common_name: Tricolor Vanda (Three-Colored Vanda, Tricolor Orchid, Suave Vanda)

planting.note: Grow in a slatted basket with minimal medium (or bare-rooted) in bright indirect to partial direct sun with high humidity and excellent air circulation; Vanda tricolor is one of the more cold-tolerant Vanda species, surviving brief temperatures to 45°F when established, and an excellent choice for zone 9b-12 outdoor orchid culture. The flowers are large (3 inches), fragrant, waxy, and long-lasting — cream-white with maroon spotting and a vivid rose-purple lip. Blooms fall through spring (Oct–Feb in zone 9). Water by misting or dipping the root mass in water daily in summer, less frequently in winter. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to partial direct morning sun in a slatted basket with minimal bark or mounted bare-root in high humidity — Tricolor Vanda grows most vigorously and flowers most freely in the open, well-ventilated conditions that allow its extensive silver aerial root system to receive regular wetting and drying cycles. Vanda tricolor is a tall, upright, monopodial epiphytic orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to Java and Bali (Indonesia), one of the parent species of many large-flowered, fragrant Vanda hybrids and one of the most cold-tolerant Vanda species in cultivation. The plant produces a robust, leathery stem clothed in two ranks of broad, strap-shaped, dark-green leaves and a large, spreading system of thick, silver aerial roots that absorb moisture and nutrients from the air. In fall through early spring, pendant racemes 8–14 inches long emerge bearing 8–15 large (3-inch), waxy, long-lasting flowers. Each flower is distinctly tricolored: the cream-white to pale yellow sepals and petals are densely spotted and blotched with maroon-brown to chestnut-brown markings, and the large, three-lobed lip is a vivid rose-purple to magenta — a striking, complex color pattern that makes the flower immediately recognizable. The flowers emit a pleasant, spicy-sweet fragrance, strongest in morning. The species is one parent of the large-flowered hybrid Vanda 'Rothschildiana' (V. coerulea × V. tricolor) and many other ornamental hybrids. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Vanda tricolor cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Vanda tricolor species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Vanda orchids for Florida outdoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Vanda (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Vestitum Calanthe (Swan Calanthe, Christmas Calanthe, White-Cloaked Calanthe)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect light in a well-draining terrestrial mix (bark, perlite, coco coir); Calanthe vestita is a deciduous orchid that loses its large, pleated leaves in fall as the pseudobulbs ripen — this is normal and expected. Reduce watering as leaves yellow in fall, then keep dry through the winter blooming period. The arching flower spikes bearing 10–25 large, white to pale rose flowers emerge from the leafless pseudobulbs in late fall and winter, making this an outstanding Christmas-season bloomer. Resume watering as new growth starts in spring. Tolerates brief temperatures to 35–40°F when dry. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light in a well-draining terrestrial orchid mix — Vestitum Calanthe grows most successfully when its seasonal deciduous cycle is respected: active growth and heavy watering in spring and summer, a gradual dry-down in fall, a dry winter rest during bloom, then resumption of watering with new spring growth. Calanthe vestita is a deciduous, terrestrial to semi-terrestrial, sympodial orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to Myanmar (Burma), Thailand, Laos, and Malaysia — a member of the large, diverse genus Calanthe which includes both evergreen and deciduous species. Among the deciduous Calanthe species, C. vestita stands out for its large, elegant flowers and its bloom timing: the arching flower spikes, 18–24 inches long and bearing 10–25 flowers, emerge from the leafless pseudobulbs in November through January — making it one of the finest winter-blooming orchids. Each flower is large (1.5–2 inches), long-lasting, and elegant: the broad, spreading sepals and petals are pure white to very pale pink, framing a large, four-lobed or three-lobed lip that is white with a distinctive orange, yellow, or rose-red callus or marking at the center — the 'cloak' (vestita, meaning cloaked) of the species name. The flowers are unscented but of a clean, formal beauty appropriate to their winter blooming season. Calanthe vestita contributed important parentage to many classic Calanthe hybrids developed in Victorian times, when deciduous Calanthe orchids were among the most popular orchids in cultivation. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Calanthe vestita cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Calanthe vestita species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Deciduous Calanthe: culture and history
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Calanthe (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Large-Leafed Pothos (Amplissimum Pothos, Silver-Stripe Epipremnum, Giant Pothos)

planting.note: Grow on a rough bark board, moss pole, or tree trunk in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil; Epipremnum amplissimum is a large-growing, fast-climbing pothos relative that can reach dramatic adult-leaf sizes in bright indoor to outdoor shaded positions. The adult leaves are long, narrow, and lanceolate (unlike the broader, heart-shaped leaves of juvenile E. aureum), in dark green with a silvery or pale green central stripe. The narrow, lance-shaped leaves distinguish this from all common pothos immediately. Best in warm, humid conditions. CAUTION: all Epipremnum contain calcium oxalate; toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow on a rough support in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained soil — Large-Leafed Pothos climbs rapidly on rough bark or moss poles and develops its most dramatically different adult-leaf form when given a vertical surface to ascend. Epipremnum amplissimum is a climbing, hemiepiphytic aroid in the Araceae family native to the Solomon Islands, Papua New Guinea, Indonesia, and parts of tropical Asia — a member of the same genus as the ubiquitous common Golden Pothos (E. aureum) but producing a strikingly different leaf form. In juvenile form, E. amplissimum produces standard small, heart-shaped pothos-type leaves. As the plant matures and climbs, the leaves transition to the distinctively adult form: long (12–24 inches), narrowly lanceolate, linear to lance-shaped leaves in dark green with a pale to silver-green central stripe along the midrib — a leaf form that looks almost nothing like the common pothos and is instead reminiscent of a narrow Philodendrum or large Scindapsus. This adult leaf form is quite unusual and ornamentally distinctive, making E. amplissimum one of the more interesting of the Epipremnum relatives for collectors of shade-climbing aroids. The plant grows rapidly in warm, humid conditions and covers surfaces quickly. Propagates easily from stem cuttings. CAUTION: toxic to cats and dogs.
sources: International Aroid Society — Epipremnum amplissimum cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Epipremnum amplissimum species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Climbing aroids for Florida shade gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Epipremnum (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Angel Wings Caladium (Linden's Caladium, White Vein Caladium, Xanthosoma Lindenii)

planting.note: Plant tubers 1–2 inches deep in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Caladium lindenii (also placed in Xanthosoma as X. lindenii) produces large, dramatic leaves in deep forest-green with strongly contrasting brilliant white veins forming a bold, graphic pattern — the primary midrib and major lateral veins are white to silvery-white, standing out vividly against the dark green blade. A different plant from the common hybrid caladiums (C. × hortulanum) — larger-leaved and producing more permanent, year-round foliage in zone 10-12 warm conditions. Protect from temperatures below 55°F; store tubers in warm conditions through winter in zone 9. CAUTION: toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Angel Wings Caladium is one of the most boldly patterned shade ornamentals available, producing large leaves with a striking, graphic pattern of brilliant white veins on deep green that is immediately eye-catching even in low-light conditions. Caladium lindenii (treated by some authorities as Xanthosoma lindenii) is a perennial, tuberous aroid in the Araceae family native to Colombia and Ecuador — distinct from the common hybrid caladiums (Caladium × hortulanum) grown from tubers as seasonal ornamentals, as C. lindenii is a larger, more permanent-growing species adapted to year-round cultivation in zone 10-12. The plant produces erect petioles bearing large (12–18 inch), broadly ovate to arrowhead-shaped leaf blades in deep, dark, forest-green. The most striking feature is the venation: the primary midrib and major lateral veins are a vivid, brilliant white to ivory-white, several millimeters wide, forming a bold geometric pattern across the dark green leaf surface — the pattern is so strongly contrasting that each leaf appears to have been painted with bright white brushstrokes against the dark ground. The white-on-dark pattern maximizes light reflection in the low-light conditions of its tropical forest floor habitat. In zone 10-12 outdoor gardens, it can be planted in deeply shaded positions as a permanent, boldly architectural shade groundcover or accent. CAUTION: toxic to pets.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Caladium lindenii (Xanthosoma lindenii) species profile
International Aroid Society — Caladium lindenii cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical caladiums and aroids for Florida shade gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Caladium (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Lady Rothschild's Orchid (Rothschild's Bulbophyllum, Red Fan Bulbophyllum)

planting.note: Grow mounted on cork or in a slatted basket with coarse bark in bright indirect to partial direct morning light; Bulbophyllum rothschildianum is one of the most spectacular of the large Bulbophyllum species, producing pendant, fan-shaped inflorescences 10–14 inches wide bearing 8–12 large flowers arranged in a semicircle — the flowers red-and-white striped with long, thread-like sepal appendages and a small, motile lip. Prefers intermediate to warm temperatures (days 70–85°F, nights 60–70°F). Keep consistently moist. The flowers have an unpleasant odor for a few days but are extraordinarily ornamental. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow mounted on cork or in a slatted basket with coarse bark in bright indirect to partial direct morning sun — Lady Rothschild's Orchid is one of the most spectacular and sought-after of the large-flowered Bulbophyllum species and rewards careful culture with extraordinary inflorescences that rank among the most unusual flower forms in the orchid family. Bulbophyllum rothschildianum is a sympodial, epiphytic orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to India (specifically the Khasi Hills of Meghalaya) and Myanmar, one of the largest-flowered species in the enormous genus Bulbophyllum (which contains approximately 2,000 species, making it one of the largest plant genera in the world). The plant produces oval pseudobulbs spaced along a creeping rhizome, each carrying a single, large, leathery, dark-green leaf. From the rhizome, pendant flower spikes emerge bearing a remarkable inflorescence: a broad, fan-shaped or semicircular arrangement of 8–12 large flowers (2–3 inches each) arranged in a precise, symmetrical arc like a peacock's tail display. Each flower has large, broadly oblong dorsal and lateral sepals in cream-white to pale yellow with vivid red to maroon striping and veining, and extraordinarily long, thread-like extensions (up to 6 inches) from the sepal tips that wave in air movement. The small lip is dark, shiny, and highly motile — moving in the slightest air current to deceive fly pollinators. The flowers produce a smell for a few days that attracts these pollinators but dissipates quickly. One of the crown jewels of Bulbophyllum culture. Named in honor of Lady de Rothschild. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Bulbophyllum rothschildianum cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Bulbophyllum rothschildianum species profile
International Bulbophyllum Society — Bulbophyllum rothschildianum: Lady Rothschild's Orchid
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Bulbophyllum (non-toxic to pets)
